response: The parabolic mirror with vertex at the origin and focus at (0, 4) follows the standard equation \( x^2 = 4py \), where \( p = 4 \), yielding \( x^2 = 16y \). For a light ray striking the mirror at point (x, y) and reflecting to the focus, the path obeys the law of reflection where the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection relative to the normal at that point. The normal vector at (x, y) can be derived from the gradient of the parabola, ensuring the reflected ray passes through (0, 4). Regarding the brightness increase, a 20% increase in light collection means the new luminosity \( L_2 \) is 1.2 times the original luminosity \( L_1 \). Using the magnitude formula \( m_1 - m_2 = -2.5 \log_{10}(L_1/L_2) \), we substitute the ratio \( L_1/L_2 = 1/1.2 \). This calculation yields a magnitude difference of approximately 0.195, meaning the galaxy appears brighter by this amount, shifting its apparent magnitude from 25 to roughly 24.805. The data demonstrates that enhanced reflectivity significantly aids deep sky observation. Detailed analysis of the data reveals that even small gains in light gathering power matter. The added data on reflectivity leads to better detection of faint objects. We must add data to our models to predict these gains accurately. The added data helps astronomers plan observations for distant targets. Data from the new coating shows a clear improvement in signal strength. The data indicates that the telescope will now capture more photons per second. Additional data confirms that the 20% gain translates to a measurable magnitude drop. The data supports the decision to use the advanced coating on the mirror. Data analysis shows that the parabolic shape remains optimal for focus. The data suggests that future upgrades could further improve performance. The data proves that the new design is effective for distant galaxy studies.
